2.1.1

Economic Growth

Economic growth — the increase in an economy's real output over time — is the most widely cited single measure of macroeconomic performance, but how it is measured and compared between countries and over time raises significant technical and evaluative questions.

Gross Domestic Product (GDP)
The total market value of all final goods and services produced within a country's borders in a given time period.
Gross National Product (GNP) / Gross National Income (GNI)
GDP plus net income earned by domestic residents from overseas assets, minus income earned domestically by foreign residents and sent abroad — GNP measures output attributable to a country's own citizens and firms, regardless of where in the world it is generated.
Nominal GDP
GDP measured in current prices, not adjusted for inflation.
Real GDP
GDP adjusted for inflation, allowing genuine comparison of output over time without the distortion of rising prices alone making an economy appear to have grown.

Worked Example — Converting Nominal to Real GDP

Nominal GDP rises from £2,200bn to £2,310bn over one year, while the GDP deflator (price index) rises from 100 to 105 over the same period.

Real GDP (Year 2) = Nominal GDP ÷ (Price Index ÷ 100) = £2,310bn ÷ 1.05 = £2,200bn
Real GDP growth = (£2,200bn − £2,200bn) ÷ £2,200bn = 0%

Key point: despite nominal GDP rising by 5%, real GDP growth was actually zero once the 5% rise in prices is stripped out — the entire nominal increase reflected inflation, not any genuine increase in output. This is precisely why economic performance should always be assessed using real, not nominal, figures.

MeasureWhat It Adjusts ForBest Used For
Real GDPInflationComparing an economy's output over time.
Real GDP per capitaInflation and population sizeComparing living standards between countries of different population sizes, or within one country over time as population changes.
Purchasing Power Parity (PPP)-adjusted GDPDiffering price levels between countriesComparing real living standards between countries, since $1 buys far more in a low-cost country than a high-cost one — a straight exchange-rate GDP comparison would otherwise understate the true purchasing power of income in lower-cost economies.
Why GDP Doesn't Fully Capture Living Standards: GDP counts market transactions only — it excludes unpaid work (childcare, volunteering), ignores how income is distributed across a population (a rising GDP can coincide with worsening inequality), does not deduct environmental damage or resource depletion, and takes no account of leisure time, health, or wellbeing. Robert Kennedy's famous 1968 speech captured this critique memorably, observing that GDP measures "everything...except that which makes life worthwhile."
Alternative/Supplementary MeasureWhat It Adds
Human Development Index (HDI)Combines GNI per capita with life expectancy and education (literacy/school enrolment), capturing broader development beyond income alone.
Genuine Progress Indicator (GPI)Adjusts GDP for factors such as income inequality, environmental damage, and the value of unpaid household labour.
The Gini CoefficientMeasures income or wealth inequality directly (companion BIZ-OMICS Income, Wealth and Poverty guide), addressing GDP's silence on distribution.
The Legatum Prosperity IndexA broader composite index incorporating economic quality, business environment, governance, education, health, safety, personal freedom, social capital, and the natural environment.
Exam Application: when asked to evaluate GDP as a measure of economic performance or living standards, always weigh its genuine strengths (widely available, consistent methodology, allows international and historical comparison) against its real limitations (distribution, unpaid work, environmental cost, wellbeing) — and bring in at least one named alternative measure (HDI, GPI, Gini) to demonstrate a fuller picture, rather than simply listing GDP's shortcomings in isolation.
2.1.2

Inflation

Price stability — low and predictable inflation — is a core macroeconomic objective, and how inflation is measured shapes both public perception of the cost of living and the Bank of England's own policy target.

Inflation
A sustained rise in the general price level of an economy over time, reducing the purchasing power of a given amount of money.
Deflation
A sustained fall in the general price level.
Disinflation
A fall in the rate of inflation — prices are still rising, but more slowly than before; not to be confused with deflation, where the price level actually falls.
UK MeasureWhat It MeasuresKey Feature
Consumer Prices Index (CPI)The price of a representative "basket" of goods and services bought by a typical UK household, tracked over time.The Bank of England's official 2% inflation target measure; uses a geometric mean, giving it a lower reading than RPI.
Retail Prices Index (RPI)A similar basket-based measure, but with a different (arithmetic) averaging method and including housing costs (mortgage interest payments) that CPI excludes.Historically used for index-linking pensions, student loans, and some bonds, though increasingly viewed as a flawed measure and being phased out of official use.
CPIHCPI plus a measure of owner-occupiers' housing costs.The ONS's preferred headline measure since 2017, addressing CPI's exclusion of housing costs.

Worked Example — Constructing a Weighted Price Index

A simplified basket contains two goods: food (weight 60%) and transport (weight 40%). Food prices rise 4%; transport prices rise 10%.

Weighted inflation rate = (0.60 × 4%) + (0.40 × 10%) = 2.4% + 4.0% = 6.4%

Key point: goods that make up a larger share of typical household spending (a higher weight) have a proportionately larger impact on the overall measured inflation rate — this is why the ONS periodically updates basket weights to reflect changing spending patterns.

Fig 1: Demand-pull inflation. AD shifts right (AD1 → AD2) along the upward-sloping SRAS curve — both the price level and real output rise together, as firms only supply more output at higher prices as the economy approaches capacity.
Fig 2: Cost-push inflation. SRAS shifts left (SRAS1 → SRAS2) while AD stays unchanged — the price level rises, but real output falls at the same time, the combination that produces stagflation.
Cost of InflationExplanation
Erosion of real incomeFixed incomes (e.g. pensions not fully index-linked) lose purchasing power as prices rise.
Menu costsThe real resource cost firms incur updating prices, catalogues, and price tags.
Shoe-leather costsThe time and effort spent minimising cash holdings (which lose value under inflation) by making more frequent trips to the bank or shifting to interest-bearing accounts.
International competitivenessIf domestic inflation exceeds that of trading partners, exports become relatively more expensive, potentially worsening the balance of payments (Section 4).
UncertaintyHigh or unpredictable inflation makes business planning and long-term investment decisions more difficult.
Why the Bank of England Targets 2%, Not 0%: a small positive inflation target provides a buffer against deflation and the associated risk of a deflationary spiral, while remaining low enough to avoid the costs above — targeting exactly 0% would leave no margin for error before the economy tips into potentially more damaging deflation.
2.1.3

Employment and Unemployment

Full employment is a core macroeconomic objective, but measuring unemployment accurately, and distinguishing genuinely involuntary joblessness from other forms of economic inactivity, is more complex than it first appears.

Fig 3: The working-age population splits into the economically active (the labour force) and the economically inactive. The labour force itself splits into the employed and the unemployed — but the inactive are counted in neither category, a distinction that matters enormously for correctly interpreting headline unemployment figures.
Unemployment
People of working age who are without a job, but are actively seeking and available for work.
Economically Inactive
People of working age who are neither employed nor actively seeking work (e.g. full-time students, long-term sick, early retirees) — they are excluded from both the numerator and denominator of the standard unemployment rate.
UK MeasureMethodologyKey Limitation
Claimant CountCounts people actually claiming unemployment-related benefits.Excludes unemployed people not eligible for, or not claiming, benefits — tends to understate true unemployment, and is sensitive to changes in benefit eligibility rules over time.
Labour Force Survey (LFS) / ILO MeasureA large household survey asking whether respondents are actively seeking and available for work, regardless of benefit claims.Internationally comparable (ILO methodology) and the UK's headline official measure, but subject to sampling error as a survey-based estimate.
Type of UnemploymentCause
FrictionalShort-term unemployment between jobs, while workers search for a suitable new role.
StructuralA mismatch between workers' skills/location and available jobs, often following long-run industrial decline in a sector or region.
Cyclical (Demand-Deficient)Caused by a fall in aggregate demand during a downturn (Section 17's negative output gap) — the type of unemployment most directly targeted by demand-side policy.
SeasonalRegular, predictable unemployment linked to the time of year (e.g. agricultural or tourism workers).
Real-Wage (Classical)Caused by a wage rate held above the market-clearing equilibrium (e.g. by a trade union or minimum wage set too high), creating excess labour supply.
Fig 4: Real-wage (classical) unemployment. A wage held above the market-clearing equilibrium (We) creates an excess supply of labour — more people want to work at that wage than firms wish to employ.
Fig 5: Voluntary vs involuntary unemployment. Holding the wage at WTU above the market-clearing We cuts employment from Qe to Q1 — this loss is genuinely involuntary, since these workers would have been employed at the market-clearing wage. The remaining gap out to the total labour force represents people who would not have worked even at We — they are voluntarily unemployed.
Cost of UnemploymentExplanation
Lost outputUnemployed labour represents productive potential the economy is not using, directly reducing actual output below potential (the negative output gap, Section 17).
Fiscal costHigher benefit spending combined with lower tax revenue widens the government's budget deficit (Section 21).
HysteresisLong-term unemployment can erode skills and work habits, making the unemployed progressively harder to re-employ even once demand recovers — potentially raising the natural rate of unemployment itself.
Social costsUnemployment is associated with worse physical and mental health outcomes, and higher rates of poverty (companion BIZ-OMICS Income, Wealth and Poverty guide).
2.1.4

The Balance of Payments

The balance of payments is a record of all financial transactions between a country's residents and the rest of the world — the fourth core measure of macroeconomic performance, alongside growth, inflation, and unemployment.

Balance of Payments
A record of all economic transactions between a country's residents and the rest of the world over a given period, split principally into the current account and the capital/financial account.
Current Account
Records trade in goods, trade in services, primary income (e.g. wages, interest, profits, dividends flowing between countries), and secondary income (e.g. remittances, foreign aid).
Fig 6: The four components of the current account. Trade in goods and trade in services together make up the "trade balance"; adding primary income and secondary income gives the overall current account balance.
ComponentIncludes
Trade in goodsPhysical exports and imports — cars, machinery, food, oil.
Trade in servicesFinancial services, tourism, education, consulting — a UK surplus area, particularly financial services.
Primary incomeCross-border wages, interest, profits and dividends — e.g. profits earned by a UK firm's overseas subsidiary, repatriated to the UK.
Secondary incomeTransfers with no corresponding good or service exchanged — foreign aid, remittances sent home by migrant workers.
Why the Balance of Payments Always Balances Overall: although the current account can run a persistent deficit or surplus, the balance of payments as a whole must always balance in principle, since any current account deficit must be financed by a corresponding capital/financial account surplus (net inflows of foreign investment, borrowing, or reserve changes) — a "statistical discrepancy" (errors and omissions) line exists precisely to reconcile real-world measurement imperfections with this theoretical identity.
Assessing a Current Account DeficitArgument
Potential concernA persistent deficit means a country is consuming more than it produces, financed by borrowing from or selling assets to the rest of the world — a structural deficit (not simply cyclical, tied to strong domestic demand pulling in imports) may signal a long-run competitiveness problem.
Less concerning circumstancesA deficit driven by strong consumer confidence and high investment (importing capital goods to expand future productive capacity) is generally less concerning than one driven by weak export competitiveness, since it can be a natural feature of a fast-growing, attractive economy drawing in foreign capital.
★ UK EXAMPLE: The UK's Persistent Current Account Deficit
The UK has run a current account deficit in most years for several decades, reflecting a longstanding trade deficit in goods (partially offset by a strong services surplus, particularly in financial services) alongside deteriorating primary income balances in some recent years. This persistent deficit has coexisted with the UK remaining one of the world's largest economies, illustrating that a current account deficit alone is not a reliable single indicator of economic weakness, and must be assessed alongside its underlying causes and how it is being financed.
Exam Application: never treat a current account deficit as automatically "bad" or a surplus as automatically "good" — always assess whether the deficit is cyclical or structural, what it is financing (consumption vs investment), and how sustainably it is being funded (stable long-term FDI vs volatile short-term "hot money" flows) before reaching an evaluative judgement.
2.2.1

The Characteristics of Aggregate Demand

Aggregate demand (AD) is the total demand for goods and services produced within an economy, at a given price level, in a given time period — the demand-side counterpart to the aggregate supply models examined in Section 3.2.3.

Aggregate Demand (AD)
The total planned expenditure on goods and services produced within an economy at a given price level: AD = C + I + G + (X − M), where C is consumption, I is investment, G is government expenditure, X is exports, and M is imports.
ComponentDefinitionApproximate Share of UK AD
Consumption (C)Household spending on goods and services (Section 6).Largest component, typically well over half of UK AD.
Investment (I)Firms' spending on capital goods (Section 7).A smaller, but highly volatile, component.
Government Expenditure (G)Government spending on goods and services (Section 8).A substantial and relatively stable component.
Net Trade (X − M)Exports minus imports (Section 9).Typically negative for the UK (a trade deficit), reducing AD.
Why the AD Curve Slopes Downward: unlike an individual demand curve (Theme 1), the AD curve's downward slope does not reflect income and substitution effects for a single good — it reflects three distinct effects operating economy-wide as the price level changes: the real balance effect (a higher price level reduces the real value of household wealth, reducing consumption), the interest rate effect (a higher price level increases demand for money, pushing up interest rates and reducing interest-sensitive consumption and investment), and the international trade effect (a higher domestic price level makes exports relatively less competitive and imports relatively more attractive, reducing net trade).
Cross-Reference to Companion Material: the mechanisms behind each individual AD component are examined in depth in the companion BIZ-OMICS Fiscal Policy and Monetary Policy guides — for example, the full transmission mechanism from a Bank Rate change through to consumption and investment (companion Monetary Policy guide) applies directly to Sections 6–7 below.
2.2.2

Consumption

Consumption is the largest single component of UK aggregate demand, making its determinants central to understanding how AD as a whole responds to changing economic conditions.

Consumption (C)
Total household spending on goods and services within the domestic economy.
Marginal Propensity to Consume (MPC)
The proportion of any additional pound of income that a household spends, rather than saves — central to the size of the multiplier (Section 15).
Determinant of ConsumptionEffect of an Increase
Disposable incomeHigher after-tax income directly raises consumption, though not necessarily by the full amount (the marginal propensity to consume is typically less than 1).
Interest ratesLower interest rates reduce the incentive to save and the cost of borrowing, raising consumption (companion Monetary Policy guide's transmission mechanism).
Consumer confidenceGreater optimism about future income and job security encourages households to spend more and save less today.
Wealth effectsRising house or share prices increase perceived household wealth, encouraging higher consumption even without any change in current income.
Availability of creditLooser lending standards and greater credit availability allow households to bring forward consumption against future income.
TaxationLower direct taxes raise disposable income directly, boosting consumption (companion Fiscal Policy guide).
★ UK EXAMPLE: The Post-COVID "Excess Savings" Effect on Consumption
UK households built up significant additional savings during COVID-19 lockdowns, when opportunities to spend on services such as hospitality and travel were severely restricted. As restrictions eased, a portion of these accumulated "excess savings" was released into consumption, contributing to a temporary consumption-driven boost to AD, alongside the demand-pull inflationary pressure examined in Section 2 (Fig. 1).
Precautionary Saving as the Mirror Image of Consumption: any factor that raises household uncertainty about future income or employment (e.g. rising unemployment, Section 3, or fears of a recession) tends to increase precautionary saving and correspondingly reduce current consumption — even before any actual fall in income occurs, since consumption depends on expected future circumstances, not only current income.
2.2.3

Investment

Investment — firms' spending on capital goods — is the smallest major component of UK aggregate demand, but among the most volatile, and disproportionately important for the economy's long-run productive capacity.

Investment (I)
Firms' spending on capital goods — machinery, buildings, equipment, and infrastructure — intended to expand or maintain future productive capacity.
Gross Investment
Total investment spending, before deducting depreciation of the existing capital stock.
Net Investment
Gross investment minus depreciation — only net investment actually increases the economy's productive capacity (shifting the PPF/LRAS outward), since depreciation merely replaces worn-out capital.
Determinant of InvestmentEffect of an Increase
Interest ratesLower interest rates reduce the cost of borrowing to fund investment, and lower the return required to make a project profitable, raising investment (companion Monetary Policy guide's transmission mechanism).
Business confidence ("animal spirits")Greater optimism about future demand and profitability directly encourages firms to commit to long-term capital spending.
Economic growth / demand expectationsFirms invest to expand capacity when they expect demand for their output to grow — a link formalised in the accelerator theory below.
Access to creditWhere firms rely on external finance, tighter lending conditions (e.g. following a banking crisis) can constrain investment even if interest rates themselves are low.
Government policyCorporation tax rates, investment allowances, and supply-side incentives (companion Fiscal Policy guide) directly affect the after-tax return on investment.
The Accelerator Theory: the accelerator theory proposes that the level of investment depends on the rate of change of national income (or demand), not its absolute level — if demand is growing steadily, firms must keep investing merely to maintain sufficient capacity; but if the rate of growth slows, even while demand is still rising in absolute terms, investment can fall sharply, since existing capacity expansion plans already anticipated a faster pace of growth. This helps explain why investment is typically far more volatile than consumption or GDP itself over the trade cycle (Section 18).
Why Investment Matters Beyond Its Direct AD Effect: unlike consumption, investment has a genuine dual impact on the economy — it contributes to AD in the short run (as a component of AD = C+I+G+(X−M)) and, through net investment specifically, raises the economy's long-run aggregate supply and potential output (companion Fiscal Policy guide's LRAS analysis) — making it central to both short-run demand management and long-run growth policy (Section 16).
2.2.4

Government Expenditure

Government expenditure is both a direct component of aggregate demand and the principal tool of the fiscal policy examined fully in Section 21.

Government Expenditure (G)
Government spending on goods and services (current spending, such as public sector wages, and capital spending, such as infrastructure) — transfer payments such as pensions and benefits are excluded from G itself, since they simply redistribute existing income rather than directly purchasing output, though they influence consumption (Section 6) indirectly via their effect on disposable income.
Determinant of Government ExpenditureExplanation
The state of the economic cycleDiscretionary fiscal policy may deliberately raise G during a downturn to support AD (Section 21).
Automatic stabilisersSome spending (unemployment benefits) rises automatically in a downturn without any new policy decision, cushioning the fall in AD.
Political priorities and the electoral cycleGovernments may adjust spending priorities around elections or in response to changing public priorities (health, defence, infrastructure).
Fiscal constraintsThe existing level of government debt and the cost of servicing it (companion Fiscal Policy guide) can constrain how much a government is willing or able to increase spending.
Crowding Out — A Key Evaluative Point: as examined in the companion BIZ-OMICS Fiscal Policy guide, an increase in G funded by government borrowing can raise interest rates in the loanable funds market (more demand for borrowed funds), potentially reducing private investment (Section 7) and offsetting some of the intended expansionary effect on AD — a critical piece of evaluation whenever discussing the effectiveness of using G to manage aggregate demand.
2.2.5

Net Trade

Net trade — exports minus imports — is the final component of aggregate demand, and the component most directly connected to a country's international competitiveness and exchange rate.

Net Trade (X − M)
The value of exports minus the value of imports — a positive net trade balance adds to AD, while a negative balance (a trade deficit) subtracts from it.
Determinant of Net TradeEffect of an Increase
Domestic income/growthFaster domestic growth increases import spending (as households and firms buy more of everything, including foreign goods), tending to worsen net trade.
Foreign income/growthFaster growth among trading partners increases demand for a country's exports, improving net trade.
The exchange rateA weaker (depreciated) currency makes exports cheaper and imports dearer in foreign currency terms, tending to improve net trade, subject to the Marshall-Lerner condition (companion BIZ-OMICS Exchange Rates guide).
Relative inflation ratesIf domestic inflation exceeds that of trading partners, domestic goods become relatively less competitive, tending to worsen net trade (Section 2's international competitiveness point).
Non-price competitivenessProduct quality, design, reliability, and delivery times affect export demand independently of price or the exchange rate.
Why the UK Typically Has Negative Net Trade: the UK has run a trade deficit in goods in most recent years (companion BIZ-OMICS Balance of Payments guide, Section 4), meaning net trade (X−M) has typically subtracted from UK aggregate demand — partially, though not fully, offset by a services trade surplus, particularly in financial and professional services.
Exam Application: when analysing a shift in AD, always identify precisely which of the four components (C, I, G, or X−M) is changing and why, rather than referring to "AD" changing in the abstract — the strongest answers trace the specific mechanism from a named cause (e.g. an interest rate cut) through to the specific component affected (I and/or C) before discussing the resulting shift in AD itself.
2.3.1-2

Short-Run Aggregate Supply

Short-run aggregate supply (SRAS) shows the relationship between the price level and total output firms are willing to supply, holding factor input costs (particularly wages) constant.

Short-Run Aggregate Supply (SRAS)
The total quantity of output firms are willing to supply at each price level, assuming input costs (especially wage rates) remain fixed in the short run — SRAS slopes upward, since a higher price level allows firms to raise output profitably even with unchanged costs.
Cause of a Shift in SRASEffect
Change in wage costsHigher wages shift SRAS left (higher cost of production at every output level); lower wages shift SRAS right.
Change in raw material/commodity pricesA rise in global oil or commodity prices shifts SRAS left — the exact mechanism behind cost-push inflation (Section 2, Fig. 2).
Exchange rate changesA weaker currency raises the cost of imported raw materials and components, shifting SRAS left.
Indirect taxes and subsidiesA rise in indirect tax (e.g. VAT) raises firms' effective costs, shifting SRAS left; a subsidy shifts SRAS right.
Why SRAS Is Distinct from LRAS: SRAS assumes at least one factor cost (typically wages) is fixed and cannot immediately adjust — this is precisely why a demand shock can temporarily push the economy away from potential output (a positive or negative output gap, Section 17), since firms can and do vary output along the SRAS curve in the short run, before wages and other costs eventually adjust to bring the economy back towards its long-run potential, examined in Section 11.
2.3.3

Long-Run Aggregate Supply: Classical vs Keynesian Views

Unlike SRAS, long-run aggregate supply (LRAS) represents an economy's maximum sustainable output once all factor costs, including wages, have fully adjusted — but Classical and Keynesian economists hold fundamentally different views on its shape.

Long-Run Aggregate Supply (LRAS)
The total output an economy can produce once wages and prices have fully adjusted, reflecting the economy's underlying productive potential — determined by the quantity and quality of factors of production, not the price level.
Fig 7: The Classical view of LRAS. LRAS is drawn as a vertical line at potential output (Yfc), reflecting the Classical assumption that in the long run, wages and prices are fully flexible and adjust to clear all markets — the economy always tends back towards full employment output, whatever happens to AD in the short run. Here AD intersects SRAS to the right of LRAS, a positive output gap.
Diagram pending — to be added The Keynesian LRAS curve: unlike the Classical vertical line above, Keynesians argue LRAS is elastic (flat) at low levels of output, where significant spare capacity and unemployed resources exist, becoming increasingly inelastic and eventually vertical only as the economy approaches full capacity. This reflects the Keynesian view that wages, in particular, are "sticky downward" — reluctant to fall even when demand is weak — meaning an economy can settle into a stable equilibrium below full employment for a prolonged period, rather than self-correcting quickly as the Classical model assumes.
FeatureClassical ViewKeynesian View
Shape of LRASVertical at potential output, at every level of outputElastic (near-flat) at low output with spare capacity; increasingly steep, then vertical, near full capacity
Wage/price flexibilityFully flexible — markets clear automatically"Sticky" — wages in particular resist falling, even when labour demand is weak
Implication for a negative output gapSelf-correcting — the economy automatically returns to full employment as wages/prices adjust downwardCan persist indefinitely without active demand-side intervention, since wages do not fall enough to restore full employment automatically
Policy implicationFavours free-market, supply-side solutions; government demand management is unnecessary and potentially harmfulFavours active demand-side intervention (fiscal/monetary policy) to close a persistent negative output gap
Why This Debate Still Matters Today: the Classical/Keynesian divide over LRAS is not merely historical — it underpins the ongoing real-world debate over whether governments should actively intervene to manage aggregate demand during a downturn (the Keynesian case for fiscal stimulus, companion Fiscal Policy guide) or instead rely on markets to self-correct while focusing policy on supply-side reform (the Classical/free-market case, Section 22) — directly shaping the conflicts and trade-offs examined in Section 23.
2.4.1

National Income and the Circular Flow

The circular flow of income models how money moves continuously between households and firms, providing the essential framework for understanding how national income is generated, and how it can settle at different equilibrium levels.

Fig 8: The basic circular flow of income. Households supply factors of production (land, labour, capital, enterprise) to firms via factor markets, earning income in return; firms supply goods and services to households via product markets, in exchange for household spending.
National Income
The total value of income earned within an economy over a given period — equal, by definition, to the total value of output produced and the total value of expenditure on that output (the income, output, and expenditure approaches to measuring GDP all arrive at the same total).
Fig 9: The full four-sector circular flow, adding the government sector, the financial market, and the foreign sector to the basic household-firm loop — each adding an injection into, or a withdrawal from, the flow of income.
Why the Circular Flow Underpins This Entire Sub-Topic: the framework here is the essential foundation for Sections 13–15 — injections and withdrawals (Section 13) are precisely the flows into and out of this circular model, the equilibrium level of national income (Section 14) is the point where these flows balance, and the multiplier (Section 15) describes how an initial change in one flow ripples repeatedly around the circle before settling at a new equilibrium.
2.4.2

Injections and Withdrawals

Money continuously leaves and re-enters the circular flow through three pairs of matching channels — understanding these is essential to understanding how national income can rise, fall, or settle into equilibrium.

Withdrawals (Leakages)
Income that leaves the circular flow rather than being spent on domestically-produced goods and services: savings (S), taxation (T), and imports (M).
Injections
Spending that enters the circular flow from outside the household-firm loop: investment (I), government spending (G), and exports (X).
WithdrawalCorresponding InjectionHow They Connect
Savings (S)Investment (I)Savings flow through financial markets (companion BIZ-OMICS Financial Markets guide) to fund firms' investment spending.
Taxation (T)Government spending (G)Tax revenue funds government expenditure (companion Fiscal Policy guide).
Imports (M)Exports (X)A country's import spending is financed in part by the export earnings its trading partners use to purchase in return.
Why the Correspondence Isn't Automatic: although each withdrawal has a natural corresponding injection, there is no guarantee that S=I, T=G, or X=M hold exactly at any given moment — it is precisely when total withdrawals and total injections diverge that national income moves away from its current level, as examined in Section 14.
2.4.3

The Equilibrium Level of Real National Output

National income settles at equilibrium when total planned injections equal total planned withdrawals — but as the Keynesian model in Section 11 makes clear, this equilibrium need not occur at full employment output.

Equilibrium National Income
The level of national income at which planned injections (I+G+X) equal planned withdrawals (S+T+M) — at this point, national income has no inherent tendency to change further.
1
Withdrawals exceed injections

More spending is leaking out of the circular flow (via saving, tax, or imports) than is being injected back in (via investment, government spending, or exports).

2
Aggregate demand falls short of current national income

Firms find they cannot sell all the output corresponding to the current level of income being generated.

3
Firms respond by cutting output and, potentially, employment

Unsold stock and falling orders lead firms to reduce production to match the weaker level of demand.

4
National income falls to a new, lower equilibrium

As income falls, so do savings, tax payments, and import spending (each depends on income) — withdrawals fall back towards the new, lower level of injections, and a new equilibrium is reached, potentially well below full employment output.

Why This Matters for the Classical/Keynesian Debate (Section 11): this model demonstrates precisely the Keynesian concern examined in Section 11 — an economy can settle into a genuine equilibrium (no further inherent tendency to change) at a level of national income below full employment, since nothing in this framework guarantees injections and withdrawals will balance exactly at the full-employment level of output.
2.4.4

The Multiplier

The multiplier describes how an initial change in injections (or withdrawals) produces a proportionately larger final change in national income, as the initial change ripples repeatedly around the circular flow.

The Multiplier
The ratio of the final change in national income to the initial change in injections that caused it — a multiplier greater than 1 means the final impact on national income exceeds the size of the initial injection.
Marginal Propensity to Withdraw (MPW)
The proportion of any additional pound of income that leaks out of the circular flow via saving, taxation, or imports (MPW = MPS + MPT + MPM).

Worked Example — Calculating the Multiplier

The marginal propensity to consume (MPC) is 0.6, meaning households spend 60p of every additional £1 of income domestically. Government increases spending by £10bn.

Multiplier = 1 ÷ MPW = 1 ÷ (1 − MPC) = 1 ÷ (1 − 0.6) = 1 ÷ 0.4 = 2.5
Final change in national income = Initial injection × Multiplier = £10bn × 2.5 = £25bn

Key point: the initial £10bn of government spending ultimately raises national income by £25bn once the full round-by-round respending effect works through the economy — the extra £15bn arises because each recipient of the initial spending in turn spends some of it, and so on, though each successive round is smaller as some income continually leaks out via saving, tax, and imports.

RoundAdditional SpendingCumulative Total
Initial injection£10.00bn£10.00bn
Round 2 (60% respent)£6.00bn£16.00bn
Round 3 (60% of £6bn)£3.60bn£19.60bn
Round 4£2.16bn£21.76bn
...continuing to convergence...£25.00bn
Factor Affecting the Size of the MultiplierEffect
Marginal propensity to withdraw (MPW)A higher MPW (more leakage via saving, tax, or imports at each round) produces a smaller multiplier, since less of each round is respent domestically.
The state of the economic cycleThe multiplier tends to be larger during a recession with spare capacity (companion Fiscal Policy guide's IMF evidence, Section 21), since firms can expand output to meet the additional demand without hitting capacity constraints or triggering inflation.
Openness of the economyA more open economy, with a higher marginal propensity to import, leaks more of each spending round abroad, reducing the domestic multiplier.
Real-World Evidence — The IMF's 2012 Multiplier Re-Estimate: as examined in the companion BIZ-OMICS Fiscal Policy guide, the IMF's October 2012 World Economic Outlook found that fiscal multipliers during 2010–12 had been considerably larger (around 1.5) than the smaller multiplier (around 0.5) originally assumed when designing post-2008 austerity programmes — meaning each £1 of spending cuts contracted the economy by roughly £1.50, substantially more than forecast, and a key piece of real-world evidence in the continuing debate over fiscal multiplier size during a downturn (Section 23).
2.5.1

Causes of Economic Growth

Economic growth can arise from two fundamentally different sources: using existing resources more fully (a movement towards the economy's current productive frontier), or genuinely expanding what the economy is capable of producing (shifting that frontier outward).

Fig 10: An outward shift of the production possibility frontier, from PPF1 to PPF2 — representing a genuine increase in the economy's productive potential, the source of long-run (potential) economic growth.
Type of GrowthCausePPF/AD-AS Representation
Actual growth (short-run)Using previously idle or underused resources more fully, closing a negative output gap (Section 17).A movement from inside the PPF towards the frontier; AD rising towards LRAS.
Potential growth (long-run)An increase in the quantity or quality of factors of production.An outward shift of the whole PPF (Fig. 10); LRAS shifting right.
Source of Potential GrowthExplanation
Increase in quantity of labourPopulation growth, higher labour force participation, or net inward migration expand the available workforce.
Increase in quality of labourImproved education, training, and skills raise output per worker (labour productivity).
Increase in quantity of capitalNet investment (Section 7) adds to the economy's capital stock, beyond simply replacing depreciation.
Increase in quality of capitalTechnological progress allows existing capital to produce more output, or enables entirely new production methods.
Discovery of new natural resourcesNewly viable natural resources (e.g. new energy sources) directly expand productive potential.
Improvements in enterprise/institutionsStronger property rights, more efficient regulation, and greater entrepreneurial activity can raise the productivity of existing factors.
Why Distinguishing Actual from Potential Growth Matters: a period of strong measured GDP growth can reflect either type — closing a negative output gap through stronger demand (Section 17), or a genuine expansion of the economy's underlying capacity. Only the second type can continue indefinitely without generating inflationary pressure, since actual growth that runs ahead of potential growth eventually pushes the economy into a positive output gap and demand-pull inflation (Section 2, Fig. 1).
2.5.2

Output Gaps

An output gap measures the difference between an economy's actual output and its potential (full-capacity) output — a central concept linking short-run demand conditions to inflationary or deflationary pressure.

Output Gap
The difference between actual real GDP and potential (full-employment) real GDP, usually expressed as a percentage of potential GDP.
Positive Output Gap
Actual output exceeds potential output — the economy is temporarily "overheating," typically associated with demand-pull inflationary pressure (Section 2).
Negative Output Gap
Actual output falls short of potential output — spare capacity exists in the economy, typically associated with easing inflationary pressure and cyclical unemployment (Section 3).
Fig 11: A negative output gap. AD intersects SRAS to the left of LRAS, leaving the economy producing below potential output, with spare capacity easing inflationary pressure — directly linked to the cyclical unemployment examined in Section 3.
Why Output Gaps Are Difficult to Measure in Practice: potential output itself cannot be directly observed — it must be estimated using assumptions about the "normal" or trend level of productivity, capital stock, and labour force participation, meaning published output gap estimates are subject to significant uncertainty and are often revised substantially after the fact, a genuine limitation when using the concept to guide real-time policy decisions (Sections 21–22).
2.5.3

The Trade (Business) Cycle

Real-world economies do not grow smoothly — output fluctuates in a recurring pattern known as the trade cycle (or business cycle), moving through distinct phases relative to the underlying long-run growth trend.

Trade (Business) Cycle
The recurring pattern of fluctuations in real GDP around its long-run trend, moving through four broad phases: boom, downturn/recession, slump, and recovery.
Recession
Conventionally defined in the UK as two or more consecutive quarters of negative real GDP growth.
Fig 12: The trade (business) cycle. Actual real GDP (solid line) fluctuates around the long-run trend growth path (dashed line), moving through recurring phases: a boom (actual output above trend, a positive output gap), a downturn or recession (falling output), a slump (output below trend, a negative output gap, typically with high unemployment), and a recovery (output rising back towards and beyond trend) — before the cycle repeats.
PhaseCharacteristicsOutput Gap
BoomHigh growth, low unemployment, rising inflationary pressure, strong consumer and business confidence.Positive (Section 17)
Downturn/RecessionFalling or negative growth, rising unemployment, falling confidence.Narrowing towards negative
SlumpOutput well below potential, high unemployment, weak or falling inflation.Negative (Section 17)
RecoveryGrowth resumes, unemployment begins to fall, confidence returns.Narrowing towards zero, potentially turning positive
Why Policymakers Try to Smooth the Cycle: both demand-side policy (Section 21) and, to a lesser extent, supply-side policy (Section 22) are frequently used with the explicit aim of moderating the amplitude of the trade cycle — cooling booms before they generate excessive inflation, and supporting demand during slumps to limit unemployment and lost output — rather than eliminating the cycle's existence entirely, which even active policy has never fully achieved in practice.
2.5.4

The Impact of Economic Growth

Sustained economic growth is a core macroeconomic objective, but it carries genuine costs alongside its benefits, and its effects are not distributed evenly across a population.

Benefits of Economic Growth

  • Rising average incomes and living standards over time.
  • Higher tax revenue at a given tax rate, funding public services without needing to raise rates.
  • Lower cyclical unemployment as firms expand output and hiring (Section 3).
  • Increased business investment and innovation, as firms respond to growing demand.
  • Improved government finances, as growth typically reduces the budget deficit relative to GDP (companion Fiscal Policy guide).

Costs of Economic Growth

  • Environmental costs — resource depletion, pollution, and carbon emissions typically rise alongside output, particularly where growth is not decoupled from environmental impact.
  • Risk of demand-pull inflation if actual growth persistently outpaces potential growth (a sustained positive output gap, Section 17).
  • Growth can worsen income inequality if its benefits are not evenly distributed across the population (companion BIZ-OMICS Income, Wealth and Poverty guide).
  • Structural change accompanying growth (e.g. deindustrialisation) can generate concentrated regional unemployment even as national output rises (Section 3).
  • Resource depletion may compromise future growth potential if not managed sustainably.
Why "Growth" and "Development" Are Not the Same: as examined in the companion BIZ-OMICS Economic Development and Growth guide, rising GDP does not automatically translate into improved human development, health, or quality of life — the distinction between growth (a rise in output) and development (a broader improvement in living standards and human welfare) matters both for advanced economies weighing growth against environmental and distributional costs, and for developing economies where growth without development is a well-documented phenomenon.
Exam Application: when evaluating whether economic growth is desirable, always specify what kind of growth (actual vs potential, Section 16), how it is distributed, and what it costs environmentally — a generic "growth is good" answer misses the nuance examiners reward, since the desirability of growth depends heavily on its composition and consequences, not simply its measured rate.
2.6.1

Macroeconomic Objectives

Governments typically pursue four core macroeconomic objectives simultaneously — but as Section 23 examines in depth, these objectives frequently conflict with one another, making trade-offs an unavoidable feature of macroeconomic policy.

ObjectiveTypical UK Target/GoalCovered In
Economic growthSteady, sustainable growth in real GDP, avoiding excessive boom-bust volatility.Sections 1, 16–19
Low and stable inflationThe Bank of England's 2% CPI target.Section 2
Low unemploymentMinimising involuntary and cyclical unemployment, ideally at or near the natural rate.Section 3
A satisfactory balance of payments positionAvoiding an unsustainable current account deficit, or excessive dependence on volatile capital inflows to finance it.Section 4
Additional Objectives Sometimes Considered: beyond these four core objectives, governments may also pursue balanced regional development, environmental sustainability, and reduced income inequality — objectives that can interact with, and sometimes conflict with, the four core objectives above in ways examined throughout this sub-topic.
Why Objectives Alone Don't Determine Policy: agreeing on these objectives in the abstract is the easy part — the genuinely contested territory in macroeconomics lies in deciding which policy tools to use (Sections 21–22), how to weigh objectives against each other when they conflict (Section 23), and how much weight to give market-based versus interventionist solutions — reflecting the underlying Classical/Keynesian divide examined in Section 11.
2.6.2

Demand-Side Policies

Demand-side policies — fiscal and monetary policy — aim to manage the level of aggregate demand directly, targeting the output gap (Section 17) examined throughout this theme.

Fiscal Policy
The use of government spending and taxation to influence aggregate demand, output, employment, and inflation — set by HM Treasury and announced by the Chancellor.
Monetary Policy
The use of interest rates (and quantitative easing) to influence aggregate demand and inflation — set independently by the Bank of England's Monetary Policy Committee.
PolicyExpansionary ToolContractionary Tool
Fiscal policyIncreased government spending; tax cutsReduced government spending; tax rises
Monetary policyInterest rate cuts; quantitative easingInterest rate rises; quantitative tightening
Monetary transmission mechanism (Bank Rate cut): Bank Rate falls → commercial banks reduce mortgage, loan and credit rates → borrowing becomes cheaper and saving less attractive → consumer spending (C) rises → businesses face a lower cost of capital, making more investment projects (I) profitable → aggregate demand rises (AD = C + I + G + (X−M)) → the multiplier (Section 15) amplifies the initial effect → demand-pull pressure increases, moving inflation towards target.
Fiscal expansion in a recession: economy is in recession with a negative output gap (Section 17) → government raises spending and/or cuts taxes → for a tax cut, households and firms have higher disposable income and after-tax profit → consumption and investment rise; for a spending rise, G is itself a direct AD component → aggregate demand rises → the multiplier (Section 15) amplifies the initial injection → output and employment rise → automatic stabilisers (rising tax revenue, falling benefit spending as incomes recover) partially offset the fiscal cost → the negative output gap narrows.
Crowding Out — A Key Limitation of Fiscal Expansion: government borrowing to fund a fiscal expansion adds to demand for loanable funds; if the supply of savings is relatively fixed in the short run, this can push up interest rates, raising the cost of borrowing for private firms and reducing private investment — offsetting some or all of the intended expansionary effect on AD. This effect is weaker when the economy has substantial spare capacity (a large negative output gap), since idle savings are more readily available to fund government borrowing without competing away private investment.
Cross-Reference: the full detail of fiscal policy tools (progressive/regressive/proportional taxation, the Laffer curve, automatic stabilisers, austerity, Ricardian equivalence) and monetary policy tools (the Bank Rate transmission mechanism, quantitative easing, forward guidance, MPC independence) is covered in depth in the companion BIZ-OMICS Fiscal Policy and Monetary Policy guides.
2.6.3

Supply-Side Policies

Unlike demand-side policy, supply-side policy aims to increase an economy's long-run productive potential directly — shifting LRAS rather than managing AD.

Supply-Side Policy
Government policy intended to increase the quantity or quality of factors of production, raising long-run aggregate supply and potential output.
TypeExamplesMechanism
Free-market supply-side policiesDeregulation, privatisation, reducing trade union power, cutting corporation/income tax rates, weakening restrictive practices.Removes barriers and improves incentives for firms and workers, without direct government spending.
Interventionist supply-side policiesGovernment spending on education, training, infrastructure, and R&D.Directly funds improvements in the quantity/quality of factors of production.
Fig 13: A successful supply-side policy shifts the whole PPF (and equivalently, LRAS) outward — the economy's productive potential increases, distinct from the short-run demand management examined in Section 21.
An Important Complication — Interventionist Policy Shifts AD Too: free-market supply-side measures typically shift only LRAS, since they involve no direct government spending. But interventionist policy — funding infrastructure, education, or R&D — is government spending, and G is a direct AD component. This gives interventionist supply-side policy a genuine dual effect: it raises AD in the short run (via the spending itself, plus the multiplier, Section 15), and only raises LRAS later, once the investment matures into actual extra productive capacity.
Evaluating Supply-Side PolicyConsideration
Time lagSupply-side policies (particularly education and infrastructure) typically take far longer to affect the economy than demand-side policy, limiting their usefulness for managing a short-run downturn.
CostInterventionist supply-side policy can be expensive, raising government borrowing (companion Fiscal Policy guide) if not matched by higher tax revenue.
Effectiveness depends on the type of unemployment/output gapSupply-side policy is most effective against structural unemployment (companion Unemployment guide) and a negative output gap caused by genuinely insufficient capacity — it does little to address a negative output gap caused simply by weak demand with capacity readily available.
Why Supply-Side Policy Is the Classical/Free-Market Prescription: consistent with the Classical view of LRAS (Section 11), free-market economists generally favour supply-side policy over demand management, arguing that the economy self-corrects towards full employment automatically, so government's most useful role is removing obstacles to that adjustment (deregulation, flexible labour markets) rather than attempting to manage AD directly.
2.6.4

Conflicts and Trade-Offs Between Objectives and Policies

The four macroeconomic objectives (Section 20) frequently cannot all be achieved simultaneously — the Phillips Curve provides the clearest formal illustration of the most famous of these conflicts, between inflation and unemployment.

The Phillips Curve
A model showing an inverse (trade-off) relationship between the rate of unemployment and the rate of inflation — lower unemployment is associated with higher inflation, and vice versa.
NAIRU (Non-Accelerating Inflation Rate of Unemployment)
The rate of unemployment consistent with stable, non-accelerating inflation — pushing unemployment below the NAIRU is associated with accelerating inflation.
Fig 14: The Short-Run Phillips Curve (SRPC). As unemployment falls, inflation rises, accelerating sharply as unemployment approaches — and briefly dips below — the NAIRU, illustrating the classic short-run trade-off between the inflation and unemployment objectives.
Fig 15: The expectations-augmented Phillips Curve (Friedman). At the same NAIRU level of unemployment, higher expected inflation shifts the whole short-run curve upward — meaning the position of the SRPC itself depends on what inflation rate the public currently expects, not just the unemployment rate.
Why the Simple Phillips Curve Trade-Off Can Break Down: Friedman's expectations-augmented model implies that in the long run, once expectations fully adjust, there is no trade-off at all — attempts to hold unemployment below the NAIRU only work temporarily, until expected inflation catches up, at which point unemployment returns to the NAIRU but at a permanently higher inflation rate. The 1970s stagflation episodes (high inflation and high unemployment simultaneously) badly undermined the simple original Phillips Curve, since it predicted this combination should be essentially impossible.
Fig 16: Has the Phillips Curve gone flat? Comparing a steeper historical curve (SRPC2) with the flatter curve observed in many economies in recent decades (SRPC1) — as unemployment falls below the NAIRU, the flatter curve shows inflation rising much more gradually, suggesting the trade-off itself may have weakened, possibly due to globalisation, anchored inflation expectations, or structural labour market changes.
Classic Policy ConflictExplanation
Growth vs inflationStimulating growth via demand-side expansion (Section 21) risks pushing the economy into a positive output gap and demand-pull inflation (Section 2, Fig. 1) if pursued too aggressively.
Unemployment vs inflationThe Phillips Curve trade-off above — expansionary policy to reduce unemployment risks higher inflation.
Growth vs the balance of paymentsFaster domestic growth increases import spending (Section 9), potentially worsening the current account (Section 4).
Growth vs the environmentAs examined in Section 19, growth often carries environmental costs that conflict with sustainability objectives.
Fig 17: How supply-side policy can reconcile the unemployment-inflation conflict. Successful supply-side policy (Section 22) shifts the long-run Phillips curve (LRPC) left, and the whole SRPC shifts down and left with it — the economy can now sustain a lower rate of unemployment at the same long-run inflation rate, effectively easing the short-run trade-off shown in Fig. 14 rather than simply moving along it.
Exam Application: when discussing whether all four macroeconomic objectives can be achieved simultaneously, the strongest answers distinguish between short-run conflicts (movements along the Phillips Curve, Fig. 14, generally unavoidable) and the possibility of easing these conflicts in the long run through successful supply-side policy (Fig. 17) — reaching a reasoned judgement about how achievable this is in practice, rather than asserting either that all objectives are always in conflict, or that supply-side policy straightforwardly solves every trade-off.
Exam Prep

Exam Technique & Model Chains of Reasoning

The Golden Chain: From Interest Rate Cut to the Phillips Curve Trade-Off

The Bank of England cuts Bank Rate (Section 21) → commercial banks reduce borrowing costs → consumption and investment rise (AD = C+I+G+(X−M)) → aggregate demand rises, amplified by the multiplier (Section 15) → the economy moves towards, or beyond, potential output, narrowing or reversing a negative output gap (Section 17) → cyclical unemployment falls as firms hire to meet the extra demand (Section 3) → but as the economy approaches and moves through the NAIRU, inflationary pressure accelerates (the Phillips Curve trade-off, Fig. 14) → the MPC must weigh the growth/unemployment benefit against the inflation cost relative to its 2% target, illustrating the short-run conflict at the heart of Section 23.

The Golden Chain: From a Negative Output Gap to Its Resolution

A negative demand shock shifts AD left (Section 17) → the economy moves to a new short-run equilibrium below potential output — a negative output gap → cyclical unemployment rises (Section 3) as firms cut output and hiring → in the Keynesian view (Section 11), wages do not fall quickly enough to restore full employment automatically, so the gap can persist → government uses expansionary fiscal and/or monetary policy (Section 21) to raise AD back towards potential output → the multiplier (Section 15) amplifies the initial stimulus → if successful, the output gap closes and cyclical unemployment falls — but if the stimulus overshoots, or coincides with weak supply-side capacity, the economy risks moving into a positive output gap and rising inflation instead (Section 2), the perpetual balancing act of demand management.

Key Exam Tips

1

Always use real, not nominal, GDP figures when discussing growth or living standards (Section 1) — confusing the two is a frequently penalised error.

2

When analysing any AD or AS shift, identify precisely which component (C, I, G, X−M for AD; wage costs, commodity prices, taxes for SRAS) is changing and why, rather than referring to the aggregate curve shifting in the abstract.

3

Distinguish actual growth (closing an output gap) from potential growth (shifting LRAS/PPF outward) explicitly (Section 16) — many answers lose marks by treating all GDP growth as equivalent.

4

For multiplier calculations (Section 15), always show the MPW or MPC value used and the formula (1÷MPW) explicitly, even when the final numerical answer is correct — method marks are awarded separately.

5

When evaluating demand-side vs supply-side policy, always weigh the time lag (Section 22) against the type of problem being addressed — demand-side policy suits a genuine negative output gap; supply-side policy suits structural unemployment or a long-run capacity constraint.

6

For questions on policy conflicts (Section 23), always reach an explicit final judgement about whether a specific conflict is temporary (resolvable via supply-side policy, Fig. 17) or a more fundamental short-run trade-off (Fig. 14) — Edexcel's less prescriptive mark schemes reward this quality of reasoning highly.

BIZ-OMICS | biz-omics.co.uk | Edexcel A-Level Economics A | Theme 2: The UK Economy — Performance and Policies
